patient.communicationStyle: INITIALLY UNRESPONSIVE. After naloxone: agitated, confused, then defensive and wants to leave. May be hostile. In acute withdrawal. Will request AMA if not engaged carefully.
patient.emotionalState: Unresponsive then agitated then defensive then potentially cooperative if treated with dignity
patient.interviewNotes: Post-naloxone interaction is the key teaching moment. Students must manage withdrawal while building rapport to keep patient for observation, assess for co-ingestion, and offer harm reduction.

responses.bystander: (Friend) We were hanging out and he went to the bathroom. He was in there for like 20 minutes so I checked — he was on the floor, not breathing right. Lips turning blue. I shook him but he wouldn't wake up. I called 911.
responses.emsReport: Found unresponsive male on bathroom floor. GCS 3. RR 4, shallow. SpO2 74%. Pinpoint pupils. Needle and burnt foil on floor. Naloxone 4mg intranasal — responded in 2 minutes. Became alert, combative briefly. RR improved to 16. SpO2 94% on NRB.
responses.postNaloxone: (Agitated, sweating) What the — where am I? What did you give me? (Retching) I feel like crap. My skin is crawling. I want to leave. You can't keep me here.
responses.ifTreatedWithDignity: (After 10-15 min) Look... I know what happened. I've been using for about 3 years. Started with pills, moved to fentanyl 6 months ago. I've OD'd once before.
responses.ifAskedAboutCoIngestion: I didn't take anything else. (If pressed) ...okay, I might have had a couple Xanax earlier today.
responses.ifAskedAboutHarmReduction: (Quiet) I'm not ready to stop. But if you have a narcan kit, I'd take one. And is there something that keeps you from getting sick when you don't use?
responses.ifAskedAboutTreatment: I'm not going to rehab right now. But if there was something outpatient, maybe.

opioidToxidrome.naloxone_response: Rapid improvement within 2 min — diagnostic for opioid toxicity

substanceHistory.prior: Prescription oxycodone — 3 years total, transitioned to fentanyl
substanceHistory.coIngestion: Alprazolam earlier today — MUST elicit. BZD co-ingestion increases respiratory depression risk.
substanceHistory.priorOverdose: 1 prior (reversed by friend with naloxone at home)

socialHistory.livingSituation: Alone, has a dog, estranged from family
socialHistory.priorTreatment: Never been to rehab. No MAT. No NA.

postNaloxone.general: Alert, agitated, diaphoretic. GCS 15. Yawning, rhinorrhea, piloerection.
postNaloxone.heent: Pupils now 6mm bilateral (DILATED — withdrawal). Lacrimation, rhinorrhea.
postNaloxone.abdominal: Diffuse cramping, hyperactive bowel sounds, nausea.

labs.value: Fentanyl POS, Benzodiazepines POS, Opiates NEG
labs.note: Standard opiate immunoassays do NOT detect fentanyl. Opiates negative because fentanyl is synthetic. BZD confirms co-ingestion.
No prolonged QTc (methadone), no Na channel blockade (TCA)

immediate: BVM ventilation if RR <8 or SpO2 <90%
Naloxone 0.4mg IV (titrate to VENTILATION not consciousness). Repeat q2-3min PRN. Intranasal: 4mg if no IV.
Over-reversal precipitates severe withdrawal and patient flight.
postNaloxone: OBSERVE MINIMUM 4-6 HOURS (fentanyl outlasts naloxone's 30-60 min → RE-NARCOTIZATION RISK)
Monitor GCS, RR, SpO2 q15min x 1h then q30min
If re-narcotization: re-dose naloxone AND consider drip (2/3 effective bolus dose per hour)
Manage withdrawal: ondansetron, acetaminophen/ibuprofen. Avoid opioids.

harmReduction.naloxoneKit: Prescribe take-home naloxone to patient AND friend. #1 harm reduction intervention.
harmReduction.mat: Offer ED-initiated buprenorphine (can start when COWS >=8). Strong evidence for engagement in treatment.
harmReduction.infectiousDisease: Link HCV to treatment (curative DAAs). HIV screen. Hep A/B vaccination.
harmReduction.socialWork: Insurance navigation, housing, outpatient treatment referral, peer recovery specialist.

keyLearningPoints: Opioid toxidrome triad: CNS depression + respiratory depression + miosis. Recognition should be instantaneous.
Naloxone reversal is therapeutic AND diagnostic.
Titrate naloxone to VENTILATION (RR >12, SpO2 >92%), NOT consciousness. Over-reversal causes severe withdrawal.
FENTANYL does NOT appear on standard urine opiate immunoassays. Negative screen does NOT exclude opioid OD.
RE-NARCOTIZATION: naloxone lasts 30-60 min, fentanyl outlasts it. Observe minimum 4-6 hours.
BZD + opioid is the most lethal drug combination — synergistic respiratory depression. Always check UDS.
ED-initiated buprenorphine has strong evidence. This visit may be patient's only healthcare contact.
Naloxone kit to patient AND bystander is the #1 harm reduction intervention.
mustNotMiss: Co-ingestion (BZD, APAP, alcohol, TCA — check UDS, APAP, salicylate, BAL, ECG)
Re-narcotization (can stop breathing in parking lot after 'successful' reversal)
Negative pressure pulmonary edema (sudden O2 requirement post-reversal)
commonPitfalls: Over-reversing with high-dose naloxone → precipitated withdrawal, agitation, patient flight
Trusting negative opiate screen — fentanyl is not detected on standard assay
Stigmatizing the patient — drives away from life-saving interventions
Not offering MAT or naloxone kit — missed critical intervention window

teachingHooks.ifGivesHighDoseNaloxone: He's vomiting, thrashing, pulling his IV. What went wrong with dosing?
teachingHooks.ifDischargesAt1Hour: He's awake, vitals normal, 1 hour since naloxone. Safe? How long does fentanyl last vs naloxone?
teachingHooks.ifDoesntCheckUDS: You treated the opioid. Did he take anything else? Will UDS even detect fentanyl?
teachingHooks.ifDoesntOfferMAT: He's alert and about to leave. What prevents the next overdose? What's the evidence for ED buprenorphine?
teachingHooks.ifDoesntGiveNaloxoneKit: His friend saved his life with naloxone. What do they need for next time?

patient.communicationStyle: Took bottle of opioid/APAP pills in suicide attempt. Drowsy but arousable after partial naloxone. Tearful, initially says accident, admits intentional when asked directly.

responses.roommateReport: I found her with an empty bottle of Percocet. She left a note. She's been depressed since losing custody of her daughter last month. Maybe 30-40 pills were in the bottle.
responses.patientWhenAroused: (Tearful) I made a mistake. I took all the pills. Maybe 30. They were my mom's Percocet. I drank some vodka too.
responses.ifAskedAboutIntent: (Quiet, tears) Yes, I was trying to kill myself. I wrote a note. I'm sorry.
responses.ifAskedAboutAPAP: (Confused) What's that? I just took the Percocet. (If explained) I didn't know there was Tylenol in it.

toxicologyAssessment.acetaminophenComponent: 9,750mg (30 x 325mg, >150mg/kg for 65kg patient = TOXIC). NAC indicated.
toxicologyAssessment.alcoholComponent: Half bottle vodka. Depletes glutathione, increases APAP hepatotoxicity. Synergistic respiratory depressant.
toxicologyAssessment.suicideAssessment: Psychiatric emergency in addition to toxicologic emergency.

socialHistory.recentStressor: Lost custody of 4-year-old daughter 1 month ago

physicalExam.general: Drowsy, arousable after naloxone 0.4mg IV. Tearful. Alcohol on breath.

labs.note: ABOVE Rumack-Matthew treatment line. NAC MANDATORY.
Mildly elevated. Peak hepatotoxicity at 72-96 hours.
Normal now. Rising INR = most sensitive early marker of hepatic failure. Trend q12h.
Alcohol depletes glutathione, increases APAP toxicity

immediate: NAC IV: 150mg/kg over 1h, then 50mg/kg over 4h, then 100mg/kg over 16h (21-hour protocol). START IMMEDIATELY.
Do NOT stop NAC for anaphylactoid reaction — slow infusion, treat reaction, continue.
Trend APAP level at 8h, AST/ALT/INR q12h x 72 hours
1:1 sitter, suicide precautions, psychiatric consult after stabilization

nacMonitoring.anaphylactoid: Flushing, urticaria, bronchospasm during loading — slow infusion, give diphenhydramine, CONTINUE NAC.
nacMonitoring.endpoint: Continue until: APAP undetectable + LFTs trending down + INR normal
nacMonitoring.transplantCriteria: King's College Criteria if acute liver failure develops: pH <7.3, OR (INR >6.5 + Cr >3.4 + Grade 3-4 encephalopathy)

teaching.correctDiagnosis: Intentional Polysubstance Overdose: Opioid + Acetaminophen + Alcohol
keyLearningPoints: Combination opioid/APAP pills (Percocet, Vicodin) — EVERY opioid OD needs an APAP level. Patients rarely know pills contain APAP.
Rumack-Matthew nomogram: plot APAP level vs time. Above treatment line = NAC required.
NAC is nearly 100% effective if started within 8 hours. Effectiveness decreases with delay. START EARLY.
Peak APAP hepatotoxicity is 72-96 hours, NOT immediately. Normal LFTs at presentation do NOT exclude serious toxicity.
Alcohol depletes glutathione AND induces CYP2E1, increasing toxic NAPQI production.
This is simultaneously toxicologic AND psychiatric emergency.
INR is the most sensitive early marker of hepatic synthetic failure in APAP toxicity.
mustNotMiss: APAP toxicity masked by opioid presentation — the APAP kills in 3-4 days without NAC. Opioid is immediate threat; APAP is the delayed killer.
Rising INR = progressing to acute liver failure
Intentional OD with note = psychiatric emergency
commonPitfalls: Treating opioid and FORGETTING APAP level — most dangerous error. Patient wakes up, seems fine, gets discharged. 3 days later: fulminant hepatic failure.
Waiting for APAP results before NAC — if >150mg/kg ingested, start empirically
Stopping NAC for anaphylactoid reaction — slow it, treat it, CONTINUE
Discharging after opioid reversal without addressing the suicide attempt

teachingHooks.ifDoesntCheckAPAP: She took Percocet. What TWO drugs did she ingest? Which one kills her in 3 days if you miss it?
teachingHooks.ifDoesntStartNAC: APAP 180 at 4 hours. Plot it. Above or below the treatment line? What do you start?
teachingHooks.ifStopsNACForReaction: She's flushing and wheezing during the NAC loading. You stop it. Now what?
teachingHooks.ifFocusesOnlyOnOpioid: Great opioid reversal. She's awake. But this is a combination pill. What's your plan for the next 72 hours?
